content.text: Password authentication remains the most widely deployed credential type in enterprise environments. Microsoft Entra ID supports password-based sign-in for cloud-only accounts, hybrid accounts synchronised from on-premises Active Directory, and federated accounts validated by ADFS or a third-party identity provider. The authentication path varies by how the tenant is configured.

attrs.alt: Password authentication modes in Entra ID

content.text: The path a password takes from the browser to validation depends on the directory synchronisation configuration:

Microsoft's recommendation for hybrid environments is Password Hash Sync. It provides cloud resilience (sign-in works if on-prem connectivity is lost) and unlocks leaked credential detection in Entra ID Identity Protection.

With Password Hash Sync (PHS), Entra Connect reads the NT hash of each user's password from Active Directory, re-hashes it using bcrypt (a salted one-way function), and uploads it to Entra ID. The original NT hash never leaves the on-premises environment. At sign-in, Entra ID compares the incoming credential's hash against the stored cloud hash — no on-premises call is required.
Entra Connect syncs password hashes every 2 minutes by default
Only the re-hashed derivative is stored in the cloud — Microsoft cannot recover the original password
Identity Protection's leaked credential detection works only with PHS — it compares hashes against known-compromised credentials from breach datasets
PHS is required for seamless SSO with Kerberos (when combined with Azure AD Seamless Single Sign-On)

# Verify and troubleshoot Password Hash Sync status via Graph and PowerShell

# 1. Check PHS sync status (PowerShell with Entra Connect module)
Get-ADSyncScheduler               # shows next sync, enabled state
Get-ADSyncConnector               # list sync connectors
Start-ADSyncSyncCycle -PolicyType Delta   # trigger a delta sync manually

# 2. Check on-premises connector sync errors
Get-ADSyncConnectorRunStatus | Select-Object -Last 10

# 3. Verify a specific user's password sync state via Graph API
import requests

def check_on_prem_sync(token, upn):
    headers = {"Authorization": f"Bearer {token}"}
    resp = requests.get(
        f"https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/users/{upn}"
        f"?$select=displayName,userPrincipalName,onPremisesSyncEnabled,"
        f"onPremisesLastSyncDateTime,onPremisesProvisioningErrors",
        headers=headers
    )
    user = resp.json()
    print(f"Sync enabled: {user.get('onPremisesSyncEnabled')}")
    print(f"Last sync:    {user.get('onPremisesLastSyncDateTime')}")
    errors = user.get('onPremisesProvisioningErrors', [])
    if errors:
        for e in errors:
            print(f"  ERROR: {e['category']} — {e['propertyCausingError']}: {e['value']}")
    else:
        print("  No sync errors")

# 4. Force a password re-hash for all users (run on DC, triggers PHS re-sync)
# This is sometimes needed after enabling PHS on an existing AD Connect deployment
# Reference: MS docs — Update-MsolFederatedDomain not needed for PHS, but:
# New-ADSyncPasswordHashSyncConfiguration (legacy) or repair AD Connect wizard

Cloud-only Entra ID users are governed by the Entra ID (Microsoft 365) password policy. Key defaults: passwords never expire unless configured otherwise, minimum length is 8 characters, and the global banned password list is always enforced. Per-domain expiry settings can be configured via Graph.

# Read and update Entra ID password policy via Microsoft Graph
# Cloud-only users follow the Entra ID (Microsoft 365) password policy
# Synced users follow the on-premises AD password policy

import requests

TENANT_ID = "YOUR_TENANT_ID"

def get_token():
    r = requests.post(
        f"https://login.microsoftonline.com/{TENANT_ID}/oauth2/v2.0/token",
        data={
            "grant_type": "client_credentials",
            "client_id": "YOUR_APP_ID",
            "client_secret": "YOUR_SECRET",
            "scope": "https://graph.microsoft.com/.default",
        }
    )
    return r.json()["access_token"]

token = get_token()
headers = {"Authorization": f"Bearer {token}"}

# Read the domain's password policy
# Requires: Domain.Read.All
resp = requests.get(
    f"https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/domains",
    headers=headers
)
for domain in resp.json().get("value", []):
    print(f"Domain: {domain['id']}")
    print(f"  isDefault: {domain.get('isDefault')}")
    print(f"  authenticationType: {domain.get('authenticationType')}")  # Managed / Federated
    print(f"  passwordValidityPeriodInDays: {domain.get('passwordValidityPeriodInDays')}")
    print(f"  passwordNotificationWindowInDays: {domain.get('passwordNotificationWindowInDays')}")

# Update password validity period
# (Only for Managed domains — Federated domains use on-prem policy)
requests.patch(
    f"https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/domains/contoso.com",
    headers={**headers, "Content-Type": "application/json"},
    json={
        "passwordValidityPeriodInDays": 90,
        "passwordNotificationWindowInDays": 14
    }
)

# Set "Never expires" for service accounts
# This is per-user, not per-domain
requests.patch(
    f"https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/users/svc-account@contoso.com",
    headers={**headers, "Content-Type": "application/json"},
    json={"passwordPolicies": "DisablePasswordExpiration"}
)

Entra ID maintains a global banned password list derived from real-world breach data and continuously updated by Microsoft. Organisations can supplement this with a custom list of up to 1,000 terms relevant to their brand, location, or industry. Terms are matched using fuzzy matching — 'P@ssw0rd', 'P4ssword', and 'password1' all match 'password'.

# Configure Entra ID custom banned password list
# Entra ID blocks passwords containing words from the global banned list plus your custom list
# Requires: Security Administrator or Authentication Policy Administrator

import requests

token = get_token()
headers = {"Authorization": f"Bearer {token}", "Content-Type": "application/json"}

# Read current banned password policy
resp = requests.get(
    "https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/settings",
    headers=headers
)
settings = resp.json().get("value", [])
pwd_settings = next((s for s in settings if s.get("displayName") == "Password Rule Settings"), None)
if pwd_settings:
    for kv in pwd_settings.get("values", []):
        print(f"  {kv['name']}: {kv['value']}")

# Update banned password list
# The settingTemplateId for Password Rule Settings is 5cf42378-d67d-4f36-ba46-e8b86229381d
# (may vary — read from /groupSettingTemplates first to confirm)
template_id = "5cf42378-d67d-4f36-ba46-e8b86229381d"

patch_body = {
    "templateId": template_id,
    "values": [
        {"name": "EnableBannedPasswordCheck", "value": "True"},
        {"name": "EnableBannedPasswordCheckOnPremises", "value": "True"},
        # Lockout threshold: number of failed attempts before lockout
        {"name": "LockoutThreshold", "value": "10"},
        # Lockout duration in seconds (60 = 1 minute)
        {"name": "LockoutDurationInSeconds", "value": "60"},
        # Custom banned words (comma-separated, case-insensitive)
        {"name": "BannedPasswordList", "value": "Contoso,ContosoIT,Seattle2024,Summer2024,Winter2025"},
    ]
}

if pwd_settings:
    # Update existing setting
    requests.patch(
        f"https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/settings/{pwd_settings['id']}",
        headers=headers,
        json=patch_body
    )
else:
    # Create new setting
    requests.post(
        "https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/settings",
        headers=headers,
        json=patch_body
    )
print("Banned password policy updated.")

Smart Lockout automatically locks a cloud account when too many failed sign-in attempts are detected. Unlike simple IP-based lockouts, Smart Lockout tracks familiar versus unfamiliar sign-in locations per user — a user who always signs in from London has a separate counter for sign-ins from an unknown IP. This reduces false positives for legitimate users while still blocking attackers.

# Smart Lockout configuration via Entra ID admin center (Graph)
# Smart Lockout prevents brute-force and password spray attacks by locking
# cloud accounts after a threshold of failed sign-in attempts

import requests

token = get_token()
headers = {"Authorization": f"Bearer {token}", "Content-Type": "application/json"}

# Smart Lockout is configured as part of the authentication methods policy
# or via the legacy settings API (groupSettings)
# Reading current lockout config
resp = requests.get(
    "https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/settings",
    headers={**headers}
)
for s in resp.json().get("value", []):
    if "lockout" in s.get("displayName", "").lower() or "password" in s.get("displayName", "").lower():
        print(s["displayName"], s.get("id"))
        for kv in s.get("values", []):
            if "lockout" in kv["name"].lower():
                print(f"  {kv['name']}: {kv['value']}")

# Default Smart Lockout behaviour:
#   Threshold: 10 failed attempts
#   Lockout duration: increases exponentially with each successive lockout
#   Familiar locations: Entra ID tracks "familiar" IP addresses per user
#     — the counter for familiar locations is separate from unfamiliar ones

# Best practices
print("""
Smart Lockout defaults:
  Threshold:              10 failed attempts
  Lockout duration:       1 minute initially; doubles each time
  Separate counters:      familiar vs unfamiliar locations
  
Tuning guidance:
  - Don't set threshold < 5 (legitimate typos cause lockouts)
  - Don't set threshold > 20 (too permissive for spray attacks)
  - Pair with Identity Protection sign-in risk policies for adaptive response
  - Monitor: Entra ID > Sign-in logs > filter Status = Failure
""")
Entra Password Protection for On-Premises AD

Entra Password Protection extends the cloud banned-password enforcement to on-premises Active Directory domain controllers. A lightweight DC Agent intercepts password change and reset operations and validates them against the cloud policy (synced via on-premises Proxy servers). This prevents weak or banned passwords from ever being set in AD, even without user interaction going through Entra ID.

# Deploy Entra Password Protection to on-premises Active Directory
# Enforces the same banned-password list on AD DS domain controllers
# Architecture: DC Agents validate passwords; Proxy Service forwards policy from cloud

# Step 1: Install the DC Agent on each domain controller (PowerShell)
# Download MSI from Microsoft, then:
# Install-AzureADPasswordProtectionDCAgent   (uses the MSI)

# Step 2: Install the Proxy Service (2+ servers for HA)
# Install-AzureADPasswordProtectionProxy

# Step 3: Register the Proxy with Entra ID
# Run on a proxy server:
Register-AzureADPasswordProtectionProxy -AccountUpn "globaladmin@contoso.com"

# Step 4: Register the DC forest
Register-AzureADPasswordProtectionForest -AccountUpn "globaladmin@contoso.com"

# Step 5: Verify deployment
Get-AzureADPasswordProtectionDCAgent    # list all registered DC agents
Get-AzureADPasswordProtectionProxy      # list proxy registrations
Get-AzureADPasswordProtectionSummaryReport -DomainController "DC01"

# Step 6: Check event logs on DCs for blocked passwords
# Event ID 10014: Password rejected by custom banned list
# Event ID 10015: Password rejected by global banned list  
# Event ID 10016: Password rejected by password complexity
# Log: Applications and Services Logs > Microsoft > AzureADPasswordProtection > DCAgent > Admin

# Audit mode vs Enforced mode
# Default is Audit mode — blocked passwords are logged but not rejected
# Switch to Enforce:
# (Done via Entra admin center: Security > Auth methods > Password protection > Mode = Enforced)

# PowerShell: read current mode via registry on DC
# HKLM:\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\AzureADPasswordProtection\DCAgent
# Key: PolicyMode  Value: 0 = Audit, 1 = Enforced

SSPR allows users to reset their own Entra ID (and optionally on-premises AD) passwords without contacting the helpdesk. Users register one or more authentication methods in advance; at reset time, they verify their identity and set a new password. SSPR is configured per tenant and can be scoped to a pilot group before full rollout.

# Enable and configure Self-Service Password Reset (SSPR) via Graph
# SSPR lets users reset their own passwords without contacting helpdesk

import requests

token = get_token()
headers = {"Authorization": f"Bearer {token}", "Content-Type": "application/json"}

# SSPR configuration is via the passwordResetPolicies resource (beta)
resp = requests.get(
    "https://graph.microsoft.com/beta/policies/passwordResetPolicies",
    headers=headers
)
policy = resp.json()
print(f"SSPR enabled: {policy.get('selfServicePasswordResetEnabled')}")
print(f"Enabled scope: {policy.get('enabledForScope')}")   # none / selected / all

# Enable SSPR for a specific security group
sspr_patch = {
    "selfServicePasswordResetEnabled": True,
    "enabledForScope": "selected",
    # the selected groups are configured in the Entra portal
    # Graph currently doesn't expose group selection in the beta policy endpoint
    # Use the portal or Set-MsolCompanySettings (deprecated) for group scoping
}
requests.patch(
    "https://graph.microsoft.com/beta/policies/passwordResetPolicies",
    headers={**headers, "Content-Type": "application/json"},
    json=sspr_patch
)

# SSPR authentication methods (configured per method in authenticationMethodsPolicy)
# Recommended: require 2 methods for reset, 1 for unlock
# Supported SSPR methods: email, mobile phone (SMS), office phone, security questions,
#                         Microsoft Authenticator push, OATH hardware/software tokens

# Monitor SSPR usage via audit logs
audit_resp = requests.get(
    "https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/auditLogs/directoryAudits"
    "?$filter=category eq 'SelfServicePasswordManagement'&$top=20"
    "&$orderby=activityDateTime desc",
    headers=headers
)
for event in audit_resp.json().get("value", []):
    actor = event.get("initiatedBy", {}).get("user", {}).get("userPrincipalName", "?")
    print(f"{event['activityDateTime'][:19]}  {event['activityDisplayName']}  {actor}  {event['result']}")
SSPR writeback (to on-premises AD) requires Entra Connect with the writeback feature enabled and Entra ID P1
Require 2 authentication methods for reset to prevent account takeover via a single compromised method
Security questions are the weakest SSPR method — prefer Authenticator app or SMS
Monitor SSPR events via Entra admin center: Identity > Users > Password reset > Activity

Password spray attacks target many accounts with a small number of common passwords to stay below per-account lockout thresholds. Smart Lockout mitigates this, but proactive detection via sign-in log analysis can identify spray campaigns in progress and trigger incident response before accounts are compromised.

Service accounts that use password authentication should have their passwords rotated on a regular schedule with cryptographically random 64-character values stored in Azure Key Vault. This eliminates the risk of long-lived static credentials and ensures the password is never stored in code or configuration files.

# Automate cloud-only user password rotation for service accounts
# Service accounts should have long, random passwords rotated on a schedule
# Store the new credential in Key Vault after rotation

import secrets, string, requests

TENANT_ID = "YOUR_TENANT_ID"
KEY_VAULT_NAME = "your-keyvault"

def get_token(scope="https://graph.microsoft.com/.default"):
    resp = requests.post(
        f"https://login.microsoftonline.com/{TENANT_ID}/oauth2/v2.0/token",
        data={
            "grant_type": "client_credentials",
            "client_id": "YOUR_APP_ID",
            "client_secret": "YOUR_ROTATION_SECRET",
            "scope": scope,
        }
    )
    return resp.json()["access_token"]

def generate_strong_password(length=64):
    alphabet = string.ascii_letters + string.digits + "!@#$%^&*()-_=+"
    # Ensure at least one of each required char class
    pwd = [
        secrets.choice(string.ascii_uppercase),
        secrets.choice(string.ascii_lowercase),
        secrets.choice(string.digits),
        secrets.choice("!@#$%^&*()-_=+"),
    ]
    pwd += [secrets.choice(alphabet) for _ in range(length - 4)]
    secrets.SystemRandom().shuffle(pwd)
    return "".join(pwd)

def rotate_service_account_password(upn, secret_name):
    graph_token = get_token("https://graph.microsoft.com/.default")
    kv_token = get_token(f"https://{KEY_VAULT_NAME}.vault.azure.net/.default")
    
    new_pwd = generate_strong_password(64)
    
    # 1. Set new password in Entra ID
    resp = requests.patch(
        f"https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/users/{upn}",
        headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {graph_token}", "Content-Type": "application/json"},
        json={
            "passwordProfile": {
                "password": new_pwd,
                "forceChangePasswordNextSignIn": False,  # service accounts don't sign in interactively
            },
            "passwordPolicies": "DisablePasswordExpiration",
        }
    )
    if resp.status_code not in (200, 204):
        raise RuntimeError(f"Password reset failed: {resp.status_code} {resp.text}")
    
    # 2. Store new password in Key Vault
    kv_resp = requests.put(
        f"https://{KEY_VAULT_NAME}.vault.azure.net/secrets/{secret_name}?api-version=7.4",
        headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {kv_token}", "Content-Type": "application/json"},
        json={"value": new_pwd, "attributes": {"enabled": True}}
    )
    if kv_resp.status_code != 200:
        raise RuntimeError(f"Key Vault write failed: {kv_resp.status_code} {kv_resp.text}")
    
    print(f"Rotated password for {upn} and stored to KV secret '{secret_name}'")

rotate_service_account_password("svc-etl@contoso.com", "svc-etl-password")

For new service accounts, prefer Managed Identities or Workload Identity Federation over password-based service accounts. Reserve password rotation for legacy integrations where managed identity is not yet supported.

Legacy authentication protocols (IMAP, POP3, SMTP AUTH, Exchange ActiveSync Basic Auth) do not support modern authentication or MFA. A single compromised password over a legacy protocol can bypass all MFA Conditional Access policies. Before blocking, audit which users still rely on legacy auth, communicate with them, and migrate their clients to modern auth.
Update application to use OAuth token instead of username/password
Update mobile device profiles; enforce via Intune
Migrate EWS calls to equivalent Graph API endpoints
Update scripts to use -UserPrincipalName and device code flow

Password authentication in Entra ID is more than a simple credential check — it encompasses the authentication path (PHS, PTA, cloud-only), the password policy (expiry, complexity, banned words, Smart Lockout), and the lifecycle management tools (SSPR, password writeback). For hybrid organisations, Password Hash Sync is the recommended path: it provides cloud resilience, unlocks Identity Protection's leaked credential detection, and requires no on-premises infrastructure at sign-in time.
PHS stores a bcrypt-hashed derivative in the cloud — not the original password or NT hash
Smart Lockout tracks familiar vs unfamiliar sign-in locations separately per user
Custom banned passwords are fuzzy-matched — variants of banned terms are also blocked
Entra Password Protection extends cloud policy enforcement to on-premises AD domain controllers
SSPR requires pre-registration; require 2 methods and prefer Authenticator over security questions
Leaked credential detection via Identity Protection requires Password Hash Sync
